multiple choice. in the United States, the main difference in the delegate model vs the trustee model for a legislator is:

The trustee model expects the legislator to follow the wishes of their constituents, the delegate model expects the legislator to make decisions that have their constituents best interests in mind

The trustee model is more popular in larger population states, the delegate model is more popular in smaller population states

The delegate model expects the legislator to follow the wishes of their constituents, the trustee model expects the legislator to make decisions that have their constituents best interests in mind

The delegate model is more popular in larger population states, the trustee model is more popular in smaller population states
The delegate model expects the legislator to follow the wishes of their constituents, the trustee model expects the legislator to make decisions that have their constituents best interests in mind.

The reason that states are divided into voting districts is:

To ensure that political parties can't unfairly influence elections

To ensure legislators represent the same number of people in a state

To ensure equality between red and blue districts

To follow the model that the federal government established for presidential elections
To ensure legislators represent the same number of people in a state.

The supremacy clause of the Constitution means:

Older states laws take precedence over newer state laws

Residents of states are subject to the laws of all levels of government

The Supreme Court gets to decide on the application of laws on the federal and state levels

Federal laws take precedence over state laws
Federal laws take precedence over state laws.

One unique law that Florida might pass that would not be needed in Oklahoma would be:

State gas taxes

Education standards for teacher licenses

Regulations for pollution on beaches

State income tax
Regulations for pollution on beaches.

States can tax citizens for anything except:

Services people use

Permits from vehicle and business licenses

Social security contributions

Incomes
Social security contributions.

Which state is most likely to have a tobacco-growing industry?

Montana

Utah

Idaho

Colorado
Idaho.
an economy is what

An economy is a system of production, distribution, and consumption of goods and services in a country or region. It involves the interaction between households, businesses, and government, and includes various factors such as economic policies, trade, and financial institutions. An economy can be characterized by its level of development, type of market structure, resource allocation, and macroeconomic indicators such as inflation, unemployment, and GDP.
what is gross domestic production

Gross Domestic Production (GDP) is a measure of the market value of all goods and services produced within a country's borders in a given period of time, usually a year. It represents the total output of a country's economy and is commonly used as an indicator of economic growth or contraction. GDP includes all final goods and services produced in the economy and accounts for government spending, private consumption, investment, and net exports (exports minus imports). GDP is calculated by adding up the value of all goods and services produced, adjusted for inflation and net of intermediate goods used in the production process.

An economy is:

How people make money

The Gross Domestic Product of a country

All the jobs and services in a country

The way goods and services are produced and consumed
The way goods and services are produced and consumed.

What role does the government play in a market economy?

They do not tell producers what to make and have few limits on what consumers can buy.

They determine the physical boundaries and regulations of the market.

They regulate what producers make and what consumers buy.

They do not tell consumers what to make and have few limits on what producers can buy.
They determine the physical boundaries and regulations of the market.

What situation best illustrates opportunity cost in an economy?

Josh spends $50 investing in stocks. The opportunity cost is the money spent on stocks instead of food.

Grace decides to sell candy and glow sticks for a fundraiser. The opportunity cost is the money spent on buying the candy and glow sticks.

Shelly decides to go to college instead of a fast food job. The opportunity cost is the time spent learning instead of working.

Scott spends three hours and $20 at the movies the night before an exam. The opportunity cost is time spent studying and that money to spend on something else
Shelly decides to go to college instead of a fast food job. The opportunity cost is the time spent learning instead of working.
what is opportunity cost

Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative foregone in order to pursue a certain action. It is the forgone benefit or satisfaction that could have been obtained if one choice was made over another. In short, opportunity cost is what is given up in order to choose a particular option. For example, if a student decides to go to a movie the night before an exam, the opportunity cost would be the studying time that they gave up, along with the possibility of a lower grade on the exam due to less preparation.

What situation best illustrates opportunity cost in the United States economy?

Josh spends $50 investing in stocks. The opportunity cost is the money spent on stocks instead of food.

Grace decides to sell candy and glow sticks for a fundraiser. The opportunity cost is the money spent on buying the candy and glow sticks.

Shelly decides to go to college instead of a fast food job. The opportunity cost is the time spent learning instead of working.

Scott spends three hours and $20 at the movies the night before an exam. The opportunity cost is time spent studying and that money to spend on something else
Shelly decides to go to college instead of a fast-food job. The opportunity cost is the time spent learning instead of working.
why is it not Scott spend three hours and $20 at the movies the night before and exam. the opportunity cost is time spent studying and that money to spend on something else.

The situation of Scott spending three hours and $20 at the movies the night before an exam is also an example of opportunity cost. The opportunity cost in this situation is the time and money that Scott chose to spend on going to the movies instead of studying for the exam or using the $20 on something else. Either scenario can be considered an example of opportunity cost in the United States economy.
when competition leads to innovation, what results? A) more profits B) better stuff C) less profits D) drops in prices
B) Better stuff.
when competition leads to innovation, what results?

When competition leads to innovation, the result is better products or services, more advanced technology, and more efficient production methods. Companies are driven to create better and more desirable products or services in order to attract customers and outcompete their rivals. This can lead to improvements in quality, design, functionality, and usability, as well as lower costs and more efficient processes. Ultimately, consumers benefit from these improvements through greater choice, higher quality, and more affordable prices.
what happens when a market economy is doing well?

When a market economy is doing well, it typically experiences the following:

1. Economic growth: The economy is growing, which is usually indicated by an increase in Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and a rise in the level of employment.

2. High consumer and business confidence: Economic growth and the expectation of future prosperity can lead to high levels of consumer and business confidence, resulting in increased spending and investment.

3. Increased production and consumption: A booming economy often sees an increase in the production and consumption of goods and services, which can improve the standard of living for people in the economy.

4. Improved competitiveness: A strong market economy tends to be more competitive, which can lead to better products, lower prices, and increased efficiency.

5. Higher profitability: Companies tend to experience higher profits when the economy is doing well, which can lead to increased investment and job creation.

Overall, a well-performing market economy can result in increased prosperity, innovation, and opportunity for individuals and businesses in the economy.

The main duties of the Federal Reserve are to do which of the following:

Set interest rates for private banks

Control inflation and encourage full employment.

Set interest rates for member banks

Maintain and regulate the gold supply of the United States
Control inflation and encourage full employment.

How does the Federal Reserve "set interest rates" for the whole economy?

The Federal Reserve sets rates for money they loan to banks and then banks set their rates according to what the Fed charged them.

The Federal Reserve notifies private securities firms of rates based on a wide variety of factors.

The Federal Reserve works with public banks to determine interest rates for private banks.

Buying and selling of bonds on securities markets.
The Federal Reserve sets rates for money they loan to banks and then banks set their rates according to what the Fed charged them.

Cutting spending and raising taxes is a way to balance the budget. Why have these items not happened?

Discretionary items limit the government's ability to cut spending and cutting taxes has political consequences.

Discretionary items limit the government's ability to cut spending and raising taxes has political consequences.

Mandatory items limit the government's ability to cut spending and cutting taxes has political consequences.

Mandatory items limit the government's ability to cut spending and raising taxes has political consequences
Mandatory items limit the government's ability to cut spending and raising taxes has political consequences.
why will Medicare and social security costs stay high or rise?

Medicare and Social Security costs are projected to stay high or rise because of several factors:

1. Aging population: As the Baby Boomer generation continues to age and retire, there will be a significant increase in the number of people who rely on Medicare and Social Security benefits.

2. Longer life expectancies: People are living longer than ever before, which means they are receiving Social Security and Medicare benefits for longer periods of time.

3. Rising healthcare costs: Healthcare costs have been rising faster than inflation for many years, and this trend is expected to continue. As a result, Medicare costs are also projected to rise.

4. Insufficient funding: There are concerns that the funding sources for Medicare and Social Security are not sufficient to keep up with the growing demand for benefits. This could lead to financial strain on the programs and potentially result in reduced benefits or tax increases to make up for the shortfall.

Overall, the combination of demographic, economic, and healthcare-related factors suggest that the costs of Medicare and Social Security will stay high or rise in the coming years.

The difference between Mandatory and Discretionary spending is:

Mandatory spending includes items such as the military, Discretionary spending includes items like Social Security.

Mandatory is written into federal law, Discretionary are programs the government can choose to spend or not spend.

Discretionary is written into federal law, Mandatory are programs the government can choose to spend or not spend.

Mandatory spending has contributed to deficit growth while Discretionary spending has stayed flat
Mandatory is written into federal law, Discretionary are programs the government can choose to spend or not spend.

Fiscal policy is:

The setting of interest rates to banks

The way the government regulates the amount of money supply

Regulation of the value of a nation's currency vs other currencies

Government's ability to raise taxes and spend the money it raises.
Government's ability to raise taxes and spend the money it raises.

Inflation can affect the price of all goods and services except:

Gas prices

Goceries

Clothing

Services paid on long term contracts like cell phones
Services paid on long-term contracts like cell phones.

COVID affected supply chain issues in these ways except for:

Materials stopped being mined

Shipping of goods slowed down

Workers couldn't work

Stimulus spending made up for lost wages
Stimulus spending made up for lost wages is the way that COVID did not affect supply chain issues.

Employers give a cost of living raise because:

That way paychecks will have consistent purchasing power

Housing always rises

To reward employees for hard work

Gas prices always rise
To ensure that paychecks will have consistent purchasing power.

All of these are reasons political parties would claim inflation happens except:

Fossil fuel use

Legislation passed under the parties President

Stimulus spending

Supply chain issues
Fossil fuel use is not one of the reasons political parties would claim inflation happens.
why not?

Fossil fuel use is related to the issue of climate change, but it is generally not considered a direct cause of inflation. Inflation is typically caused by factors such as an increase in the money supply, rising production costs, higher demand for goods and services, or other macroeconomic factors. While the cost of fossil fuels, such as oil and gas, may have an indirect impact on inflation due to their importance to the economy, it is not typically cited as one of the primary reasons inflation may occur.

Corporations contribute to inflation because:

A lack of corporate taxes allows them to make larger profits

They pay the highest wages so any increase in those results in more spending

As the largest employers, they pay more employees more money to attract workers

A lack of competition means price raises are easier to implement
A lack of competition means price raises are easier to implement.

All of these would be examples of unsafe working conditions that government regulates except:

Long hours

High payroll taxes

Dangerous industries

Low wages

High payroll taxes are not an example of unsafe working conditions that government regulates. While payroll taxes impact workers and employers, they are not directly related to workplace safety or health. Unsafe working conditions that government regulates include dangerous machinery, chemicals, or materials, exposure to harmful substances, excessive noise, extreme temperatures, and other hazards that can lead to injury or illness. Additionally, regulations may be put in place to require employers to provide protective equipment, training, or procedures to ensure worker safety.

Low interest rates are desirable for the economy because:

Banks can borrow less money and charge consumers higher interest rates

They help keep control of inflationary pressures

More consumers can afford to finance high cost items

Banks can borrow more money and lend to consumers

Low interest rates are desirable for the economy because they help keep control of inflationary pressures. When interest rates are low, it can stimulate spending and investment in the economy, leading to higher economic growth and job creation. It can also encourage borrowing and investment in businesses, which can help to increase productivity and competitiveness. Additionally, low interest rates can make it easier for consumers to borrow money to purchase high-cost items, such as homes or cars, which can boost economic activity. However, prolonged periods of low interest rates can also lead to risks such as asset bubbles or debt accumulation, so it is important for monetary policy to balance the benefits and risks of low interest rates for the economy.

All of these are subsidies except:

Credit

Refund

Tax incentives

Service

Service is not a subsidy. Subsidies are financial assistance provided by the government to businesses or industries to encourage or support certain activities or products. Credits, refunds, and tax incentives are examples of subsidies that reduce the cost of doing business or increase profits for certain companies. These subsidies can take various forms, such as cash payments, tax breaks, or exemptions from certain regulations or fees, with the goal of promoting economic growth or achieving certain policy objectives.

The minimum wage is an example of:

Government regulation of the economy to protect workers

Government regulation of the economy to support consumer spending

Government regulation of the economy to encourage full employment

Government regulation of the economy to control corporations

The minimum wage is an example of government regulation of the economy to protect workers. The aim of minimum wage laws is to provide a basic standard of living for workers by ensuring they are paid a fair wage for their labor. The government sets a minimum wage rate, which employers must pay their employees. This helps protect workers from being exploited or underpaid and ensures that they have a minimum level of income to support themselves. It is also seen as a way of reducing poverty and income inequality, and promoting overall economic growth by boosting consumer spending and demand.

What movement in the late 1800's led to further economic involvement by the government?

The Age of Invention

The Industrial Revolution

Mergers of major businesses to form monopolies

The rise of the railroad industry into the western half of the continent

The rise of the railroad industry into the western half of the continent led to further economic involvement by the government. The railroad industry was one of the most important and influential industries during the late 1800s and early 1900s. The expansion of railroads into the western half of the continent played a significant role in the growth of the US economy, but also led to monopolistic practices by some railroad companies, such as controlling prices, bribing politicians, and engaging in other anti-competitive activities. As a result, the government became increasingly involved in regulating the railroad industry, including enacting laws to prevent monopolies and ensure fair competition, setting rates for goods and services, and regulating working conditions. This increased involvement in regulating the economy paved the way for further government intervention in other industries in the years to come.
